FT_IC1.evidence: Data drawn from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, periods 2 and 3 follow-ups (2018–2021), 21 NIH-funded sites, n=9095 adolescents aged 10–13.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: two multivariable linear regression models estimating prospective associations of lifetime and past 12-month cyberbullying with manic symptom sum score 1 year later.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Total screen time (hours/day) appears only as a covariate in adjusted models — it is not an IV or DV with a reported coefficient for the SM↔outcome relationship (fails binding clarification: SM as covariate-only). The exposure variables are cyberbullying victimisation (lifetime and past 12-month yes/no). Step 2 not reached. Additionally, cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable under binding clarification #3 (cyberbullying alone fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient, which is absent here).

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. Cyberbullying is the IV; manic symptoms is the DV. Screen time included only as a covariate. No phone/SM variable has its own coefficient in any result location.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study Year 3 (2019–2021), N=10,054 adolescents aged 12–13, 21 research sites across the US.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: ordinal logistic regression models assessing associations between six media parenting practice categories and frequency of mature-rated video game use and R-rated movie consumption.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, etc.) has its own coefficient in any result location (main tables, appendices D–G). The DVs are R-rated movie consumption frequency and mature-rated video game frequency. Mature/R-rated content alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable (binding clarification #4). The IVs are media parenting practices (parental screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, use of screens to control behavior, parental monitoring, limiting screen time) — these are parenting/contextual variables, not phone/SM variables. Step 2 not reached. No qualifying phone/SM ↔ individual-trait pairing exists anywhere in the paper.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. DVs are content-rating categories (R-rated movies, mature video games), not qualifying phone/SM variables. IVs are parenting practices. No phone/SM-specific estimate paired with individual-trait variable exists anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study across four annual assessments (baseline 2016–2018 to Year 3 2019–2021), N=9,519, 21 study sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: compositional data analysis (CoDA) with isometric log-ratio transformations and mixed-effects models examining longitudinal changes in screen time subtypes (TV, video games, digital socializing), sleep, and sports/exercise activity over four annual assessments.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2026 online); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Digital socializing (texting + video chatting + social networking) has its own coefficient estimates (e.g., increased by 0.98 h/day from baseline to Year 3, 95% CI 0.51–1.45, p<0.001; sex-stratified: females 1.39 h/day, males 0.48 h/day). This is a qualifying phone/SM variable. However, the other side of the equation is time/wave (year) and COVID period as the IV in a growth-curve model — these are contextual/temporal variables, not individual-trait-level variables. Sex-stratified analyses use sex as the stratifying variable, which is demographic. No individual-trait-level variable (health outcome, behavioral outcome, cognitive measure, well-being, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis involving digital socializing or any other phone/SM variable. The paper describes descriptive longitudinal trajectories only; no analysis pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome or predictor. Other-side rule: all predictors are time, age (time-varying covariate), COVID period, and baseline demographics — none qualify as individual-trait.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. Paper describes longitudinal changes in screen time subtypes (including digital socializing, which qualifies as a phone/SM variable) but the only predictor is time/wave (contextual) and demographics — no individual-trait variable on the other side. The study does not pair a phone/SM variable with any individual-trait outcome or predictor.
